entry.title: Admiral Foggo In The Desert
entry.summary: In the desert, a man wants an ocean.
entry.content:
ADMIRAL FOGGO IN THE DESERT

Admiral Foggo woke up in a pile of sand. He had a terrific headache and felt nauseous. He was lost in a desert, and so concluded he had a condition called sandsickness which, though fictional, affected him badly.

He stood up carefully and slithered down the dune a little way. He sucked his teeth and rotated on the spot. Nothing but yellow sand and blue sky and the sun, high up.

'It's damn hot,' he said to himself. He wiped his sweating brow on his navy woollen Navy jacket and looked down at his navy Navy trousers and his black Navy shoes. He was bareheaded. The prince's men must have taken his white Navy hat. The sandsickness was intolerable.

'I believe it is because I am so used to the motion of the sea, I must have inverted sea legs.' Admiral Foggo, a scientific man, hypothesised, and then tested. 'I will have to walk to safety. My sandsickness is psychosomatic, a consequence of my life on the sea. Therefore, I must fool my own brain.' He pictured the lubbers he had known, who had gone green even at the sight of his first command, the sleek and lethal white cutter named Happy Pig. He imitated their wide-splayed posture, hands held out for balance, and took a couple of rolling steps. He felt the sandsickness abate a little, and looked about again. On ships, sometimes things appear over the horizon without much effort on one's own part. But he had not moved. He did not know where to move to.

'\"If one does not know to which port one is sailing, no wind is favorable". Seneca.' This was addressed to himself, not Seneca, who had been dead for 1,965 years. But he was fond of naval aphorisms. Foggo could tell north and south and up and down by the stars. He could tell Pacific from Atlantic only by the merest glance at chart and compass. But cast into the desert by the wrathful prince, he was all at sea.

'The prince is an Arab. Therefore it is likely I am in Arabia, as that is where I was with him recently. I am familiar with the Red Sea and the Persian Gulf. They tend to be south, west and east of most of Arabia. I must merely not walk north, and I shall reach a coast. No doubt I can commandeer a fisherboat and be on my way. So, let me find north, and walk from it. This repulsive desert shall be my open road, and carry me to the sea. By my lack of shadow, it is either night or midday. By the presence of the sun, midday. Its verticality also suggests I am near the equator, so I am indeed likely in Arabia.' It all fell into place. 'I will wait until it dips, for that shall be west.' He paused. 'I am sure the sun sets in the west even in Arabia. I would have heard otherwise.'

He sat back down and fell asleep, and woke in the blackest night. There was no hint of the sun at any point on the horizon.

'Ah. An error on my part.' He glanced about, making sure no one had seen him, but no one had, because he was lost in the desert. 'The North Star will do.' He located it easily, squashed between his friends the Little and Large Dippers. He turned his back to it and began to walk, and then tripped over his rucksack.

'Aha. Jetsam.' He discovered rum, half-drained, likely by villains, and a white glove. He shouldered his burden, turned his back to the North Star, and weighed anchor.

He made it fifty feet before he was bitten by a snake. He sighed. He had taken a bullet at the Battle of Santiago de Cuba, from one of his own enlisted. A snake bite was much less serious. He picked up the snake by its head and looked at it with disappointment. 'You'll regret that, serpent. My blood is more salt than iron after a lifetime on the seas. You're in for a thirsty night.' He dropped it, and it slithered away, south.

'The snake shall mark my road. It will carry a bit of me ahead of me.' Now snakesick and still a little sandsick, he advanced. After many hours of following the snake road, the sun rose on his left. Admiral Foggo called out to the snake, 'you see? I can navigate just as well here as on the seas.'

The snake ignored him. It wound sideways, which Foggo ascribed to it being salt-drunk. 'I bet you've never even seen the sea.'

He remembered saying something similar to the prince. That damn prince! All Foggo had done was praise the sea and criticise the desert. And for that, he had been exiled to the sands. He frowned and reached for the white glove.

'It's the prince's glove!' The snake was still not interested. 'I must have snatched it in exchange for my white Navy hat.' He trudged on, in companionable silence with the snake, for many hours.

'It's damn hot. Must be midday.' He paused to sip a little rum. When he looked up, the snake had gone, burrowing for cooler sand. 'Ah, you coward.'

He pressed on. The snakesickness went, and he saw a cactus that was shaped like a fat man holding a cigar in a wide hand. A small brown bundle was at the fat man's feet. Foggo approached it.

'What are you? Some sort of desert seal?' The baby camel squinted at him warily. 'Aha, I know you're a camel. Are you sandsick too?'. There was no response. 'Have some rum.'

He fed the young camel from the bottle of rum, and it mewed. He picked it up and gave it a close look. 'I suppose you want to come with me.' Admiral Foggo was taking command of the enlisted. Naval life was not solitary, and he was unused to being alone. The camel's long neck draped over his shoulder. It was heavy. But he had someone to talk to on the desert road.

'I don't know why the prince objected. This desert is dreadful.'

The camel's silence was a rebuke.

'I could have been more polite, I know.'

It coughed.

'He did invite me to his home. But why invite a naval man if you don't want to hear about the sea?'

It shivered.

'You can't be cold, sailor. It is an oven out here. Not like the open ocean. The sea breeze! This place is a land lacking the thinnest stream.'

He advanced, waddle-like, relentless as the waves. Admiral Foggo would not be kept from the water. He would march from the desert. The camel squirmed, and he put it down and gave it more rum, medicinally. He had some too, medicinally. They walked together, until Foggo paused, seeing something sausage-shaped in the sand.

'Is that a desert seal?' The camel didn't know, so they walked towards it. It was another camel, big and dead. Foggo knew it was the little camel's mother. They looked alike.

'Condolences,' he said. When the sailor who had shot him had visited him in the Happy Pig's hospital, they had discussed naval strategy to avoid recrimination. He tried the same thing with the camel.

'The prince invited me here to discuss whether naval theory could be applied to desert warfare.'

The camel did not have a strong opinion on this, or was distracted, or grieving, and Admiral Foggo knew when to be quiet. In the silence which followed, as they walked through the desert, on the clear wind Foggo's ears detected hoofbeats.

'Ahoy,' he muttered, slowly, eyes following where ears pointed. Stuffing the camel down the front of his woolen jacket, he crept to the top of a dune. A pack of horsemen, advancing. Their steeds were sweating, eyes wide and wild, foaming at the mouth. Their riders were swathed in cloth, faces obscured, but swords and lances and rifles adorned high saddles.

'A war party! No doubt come to finish me off!' Foggo dropped to his belly, squashing the camel, which mewed.

The mew carried. It was piercing, like a ship's whistle. There was a command in Arabic, and a horse reared, slowed, stopped, turned. A turbaned head with green flashing eyes turned, hands pulling at the reins. One was gloved in white. The other was bare.

'The prince! You damn fool, you've killed us both!' Foggo was accustomed to collective punishment, as when a ship goes down the sea takes no prisoners. He assumed the camel would perish too. Then he remembered where he was. He plucked the creature from his jacket and placed it down on its unsteady, foal-like legs.

'Run! Save yourself!'

The camel took two steps and tumbled.

'Goddamn this blasted sandsickness!' Foggo rose. The prince saw him, and waved his lance. The horsemen circled, and began bearing down on him. Foggo scooped up the camel and began to run, the sandsickness driving him to his knees again and again. The sun bleached all strength from him. The sand sucked at his feet. The dry air tore at his throat, and the hoofbeats grew louder. A shadow fell over him, a rider on a horse.

'Admiral, are you feeling quite alright? You had us worried!'

Foggo staggered, fell, clutching his four-legged crewmate to his heart.

'Strike! Kill me but spare my crew!' He knelt up, eyes shut, awaiting the fatal blow.

'Admiral, please! You're quite delirious.' He opened one eye. The prince was standing before him, dismounted. His horse was breathing a little heavily, but otherwise was demure. One of the prince's grooms caught up with them and fed it an apple. 'You have had us terribly worried.'

'Eh?'

The prince gave a command in Arabic, and the groom gently took the young camel, gave it water, and placed it in a saddlebag.

'Last night - I must say, I have never seen a man drink as much as you did. You were quite lively.'

'We fought, did we not?'

The prince helped him to his feet.

'Only with words. The merits of the sea and the desert. You have a poet's heart. My youngest son, I am afraid, you convinced to become a sailor with your descriptions. He is already on the train to Alexandria. You wrote him a letter of recommendation to the British attache.'

'And then what did I do?' asked Foggo, eyes narrowing. Perhaps he was beginning to remember.

'You danced, quite marvellously. You leapt over the fire.'

'While that sounds like something I am capable of, I struggle to believe it.'

'There are a hundred witnesses at my camp. Please, come back. After your dancing, we lost sight of you. We found your tracks.'

'I was in the deepest desert.'

The prince and the groom shared a glance, a pause. The camel mewed again.

'Yes, you were. You have been on quite the adventure.'

'And your glove?' Foggo reached into his pack.

'You shook my hand, called me "brother". Then fell over. The glove went with you.'

'Hmm,' retorted Foggo. He returned the prince's glove. 'Perhaps the sandsickness got to me earlier than I thought.'

Again, the prince and the groom shared a glance. 'Perhaps.'

'Sandsickness can be terrible,' offered the groom, solicitously.

'Now, admiral, please. Up on my horse. It is an open road back to the camp.'

'A what?'

'Excuse me, nothing.'

'Ah, very good.' The admiral was back in command. The motion of a horse under him was much like the sea, and he began to feel better. The camel at his leg nuzzled him. He did not see his footprints, winding this way and that, directly adjacent to the wide and well-tended road they returned to the camp on. He was a man of the sea. What need had he of the open road?

entry.title: Ajax and Irene
entry.content:
AJAX AND IRENE

'John said he lost his foreigners, and there's a storm coming.'

Peter the squire, atop Ajax the horse, rode through the mist-robed valley. Past pines, ferns and dripping firs, eyed by brown-collared, long-tailed sheep, they approached the copse where the foreigners had last been seen. It was easy to find for a native, and native Peter looked after those in his valley, though he had to be prodded to action by his wife if they weren't Welsh.

Ajax paused at the edge of the copse, preparing to enter the gloom, and Peter had to nudge him on with a squeeze. He could see the weak fire the foreigners had lit. Three horses, not three men, were gathered around it in a tight ring. The men themselves were sheltering under a tree each, and each was soaked. Two looked miserable, one waved cheerfully. The waver advanced, a thickset man with a broken nose and cauliflower ears, but the hand he offered was dainty. In his other he held a watercolour tin.

'Hello, sir. I am Dalibor.'

Peter gave the strange name a moment to settle itself in his mind. 'Hello, Dalibor. I'm Peter. I thought you might benefit from a guide down to the bridge.'

Dalibor looked hesitant, raising his paints in protest, but a second foreigner stepped forwards. 'I'm Andrej -- please, yes. Down. It is cold here!'

The final foreigner, taciturn, introduced himself as 'Franc'.

'Very good. Mount up, gentlemen.' The three rode John's horses, and Ajax knew them. Like school friends, Ajax, Ethiopia, Falstaff and Bishop rubbed together, bringing Peter a little closer to the others than he might have chosen. 'So, where have you come from? You aren't prepared for our summers, I think.'

Dalibor laughed, Andrej shivered, Franc sneezed. 'We're Slovenes.'

'Ah.' At least they weren't French.

'We're having a day trip, sir,' continued Dalibor. 'Industrial representatives sent from Ljubljana to Cardiff, but we heard of the beauty of this valley.'

It sounded like labour talk to Peter, and bore the stink of modernity, but while they were in his valley they were his guests, if not his friends, and he kept his tongue. Ajax did not, whickering at the heavy rain.

At the bottom of the valley was the bridge which led to John's, and to the train station, but the bridge was a carcass now, belly-up in the river's spate.

'If the bridge is down the road is flooded.' Peter sighed. Guests, not friends. He had a duty. 'Come on, you can stay with me and the wife. In the morning, when the weather clears, it will be safe to cross.'

Dalibor was a poor rider. 'This gelding is taking revenge on what I have for what he lacks,' he muttered to Peter. The squire held back a smile. Labour talk and crude jokes, in his valley. His father would be turning in his grave.

The first to meet the four was Peter's young daughter, Mary. She rushed up and hugged Peter, then stayed with her face pressed into his coat as fear or shyness overtook her at his strange, shivering companions. Liz dragged the pair out the way so the Slovenes could reach the fire.

'Thank you.'

'Thanks.'

'Hvala.'

But they were not rude guests. Before the first cup of tea was ready, Franc had befriended their cat, Dragon, Andrej was carefully gathering coats so they did not drip, and big Dalibor was talking amicably to little Mary. By the second cup, Dragon was sitting on Franc's lap as he and Liz ruminated on Wales's and Slovenia's places within the British and Austro-Hungarian empires, Andrej was settling the horses in the stable, and Dalibor and Mary were playing a game with paper and pencils. He even had his watercolours out. By the third cup, even Peter had grudgingly put down his newspaper and joined them at the fire.

Andrej had Ethiopia, Falstaff and Bishop ready before dawn broke on a clear day. Liz saw them fed, and Mary shed a tear when they departed. Peter and Ajax took them to a fording spot upriver, which could now be safely traversed.

'Goodbye, goodbye.' Dalibor shook his hand. Then he reached into his pocket, took out a folded note.

'No money, not necessary. You were a guest.'

'We would not insult. For you, my friend.' He pressed it into Peter's hands, nudged Bishop on, unsteady in his saddle. Franc and Andrej bowed at the waist and followed their leader. Peter did not open the letter, but watched the foreigners leave his valley. He was a good host. Once they were safely beyond his bailiwick, he unfolded the paper. Not a letter. It was a picture of his daughter, in simple black watercolour. Dalibor had captured her perfectly. Her crafty eyes, always sparkling at a trick. Her guileless cheeks, blushing before Peter could fall for it. Her new white teeth. Somehow Dalibor had even shown her blonde hair with black paint alone.

'The man was an artist,' Peter said to Ajax.

The next day someone shot an archduke, and all Europe shattered like a dropped decanter.

Ajax was the valley's first casualty. He enlisted, along with his rider. He had been spooked by a shell, shied, fell into wire, and was shot. Horses respond to war the same as men: variously. Ajax had a peaceful heart. Irene, who replaced him, loved war. She was a violent horse, grey and muscular and cruel. In Vauclerc she had bitten off the hand of a child which had tried to stroke her nose. Peter had survived two years of war on Irene, but he was just as lost as Ajax had been.

From the green soft valley of Gobowen, Peter had come to the hard red ridge outside Ripont. From the quiet of Wales to the hell of France. No verdant trees, but soil made at once fertile and rancid by blood. Irene revelled in the stench, sniffing with quivering wet nostrils, and pawed at the filth. Peter drew from her mad strength. A sabre quivered in his hand, with terror and thrill. He and his squadron would smash through the shelled lines, get among the artillery the Austrians had left out of position, and cut down the gunners.

Peter's commander, wild-eyed Lancer George, raised his pistol. 'Friends of England! Be fearless! Kill! Kill! Kill!'

Peter took up the huzzah, and Irene's hooves gouged the mud like shellfire. Grey streak, she overtook Lancer George, overtook them all, with Peter snatched along. She tore through smoke and iron rain and thickets of wire, over trenches crowded with woodlouse-grey Austrians. Peter lashed out with the sabre, catching a foreigner by the face, and Irene reared in celebration. A grenade exploded and took her eyes and she fell, horsemeat. Peter was thrown into a shell hole. He lay stunned, high riding boots in a sludge of dead men.

Red day turned to cold black night. Horses and men wept in the distance. Two pairs of jackboots crunched towards him, and he was dragged from his hole by his webbing. The jutting brows of two Austrian helmets crowded his faint and delirious gaze. Foreign words. An order. One left him, the other raised his rifle.

Peter spoke no German. 'Moment, s'il vous plaît'. His executioner paused and Peter reached into his tunic. A cigarette case, but no cigarette. Mary\'s portrait. A flare burst overhead, and her black-blonde hair shone. Like a magic painting, with every horror that Peter saw, Mary looked more beautiful. He suddenly felt no fear of death, but an invincible certainty that when the Austrian fired he would wake up in his valley. The jackboot stamped, an inch from his nose. The barrel twitched and fired. The bullet, like a million others, sank into the mud. Peter did not wake up in his valley. The Austrian crouched.

'We are called to attack.' He spoke English. 'A penal battalion for Slovene nationalist agitators.' His voice was bitter, but familiar. 'We will all die.' He put his rifle down, gently, and with slim fingers took Peter's watch, looked at it for a moment. 'When that is 10 o'clock, we are all dead. It will be safe to cross, back to your lines.' He pushed back his helmet, just a little. It showed cauliflower ears, a broken nose. He had tears in his eyes. His commander barked from the darkness. Dalibor, unsteady on his feet, left his friend in the mire of the shell hole and in the glittering light of the flare.

'One survivor from 62^nd^ squadron, a Second Lieutenant Peter Everett. Commendation recommended. Austrian counter-attack annihilated, no prisoners taken.'

entry.title: Alchemist
entry.content:
ALCHEMIST

Silverside Ray was described by those who did not know him very well as a \'proper London geezer\'. No one knew Silverside Ray very well. Silverside Ray owned a gym and a butcher's shop, adjoining each other and next to a train station which brought in City boys from Dalston and Hackney and further.

Had he any hair, it would have been silver. Silverside Ray was seventy. But he was broad-shouldered and thick-forearmed from carrying carcasses and shanks and sides of rib, and from lifting iron barbells in the gym. He had done both of these things for sixty years, hated them both, and wished for more.

The people who called Silverside Ray a 'proper London geezer' were excited by him because they felt their lives lacked authenticity, for they themselves were neither proper nor London nor geezers. They were City boys of the new school, twenty-five and thirty-five and forty-five year old boys from the Home Counties or Scotland who went to non-London universities and got degrees and then went to the City because that was how you made the money. They were men in tight, expensive, bad suits which wore thread-bare at the thighs, and for whom novelty and parody was the closest they got to authenticity: moustaches like First World War officers, which they wore as jokes. Shirts by Turnbull and Asser because James Bond wore them, and Omegas and Rolexes for the same reason, and which they ignored because they spent all day looking at screens which displayed the time precisely, synced to the atomic clock in Nevada Labs, because a fraction of a second's delay in trade volumes could cost a fortune. Their worlds felt unreal, so they wished Silverside Ray into reality.

They needed protein, to bulk, to become real and manifest like Ray, and they were constantly calculating grammes of protein per kilogramme of body. They could afford any fad powder or herb or potion. But what they really loved was what Silverside Ray sold them, which, unknown to them, was any part of the cow which Londoners did not eat. It was a good deal for Ray: he got ungutted carcasses cheap, only paying the abattoir for the flaying. He told the abattoir he pickled guts in formaldehyde for schools.

As he sold flank steak and rib out the front of his shop, he had cauldrons boiling hoof. He told the boys at the gym that Cossacks drank the jelly when they fought Napoleon, and he sold it in corked glass milk bottles, for authenticity. He cut oesophagus and trachea finely and dehydrated them in his ovens and sold them as 'protein chips'. Contemptuous of the boys' pointless maleness he peeled uterus, cervix, ovary apart. Salted and fried, he said his dad had called it 'German jerky' because he had eaten it at Sword Beach in '44. Bullock penises were easy to sell; when dogs ate them they were called pizzles. But dried and seasoned and chewy and sold as 'shin slices', the boys said it was better than chorizo.

Silverside Ray was no dinosaur: he had a smartphone, and followed TikTok fitness trends. He talked about macros, lying through his teeth about magnesium and potassium. He understood bulking and shredding. He faked sympathy with the scrawny, callow youths who knew they spent too long at their computers, and when he gave them glass jars with gingham lids rattling with dried cow brain, resembling coral growths, they looked at him like an uncle. He said it was a type of tripe, and the boys, missing home, thought of the home-cooked meals they had turned their noses up at, and they ate brains.

Silverside Ray was an alchemist. He took offal and transformed it to gold. The boys took gold and turned it to shit. The more they bought, the more he detested them. The richer he got, the more reckless.

Joey had never wanted to work in the City. He had wanted to be a doctor. He missed his grades, but was a bright boy. He got an internship with one of the banks instead. His boss, Marwood, told him about a gym. It was authentic, he was told. There were geezers there, he was told. There were lorry tires to flip, and bare concrete. When the trains passed, the weights on the rack rattled. It was proper. Joey wanted this authenticity: he had wanted to be in Medecins San Frontieres, he told himself, now this was no longer a possibility.

Better people than Joey were fooled by Silverside Ray. On the first of the month Ray sold prairie oysters: why would a man cheerfully and overtly selling bull testicles lie about the German jerky? So when Joey warned Marwood that he was eating bovine uterus, that he could tell by the distinctive cilia, Marwood got angry, and refused to believe him. Marwood told Ray and they laughed at the boy Joey. When Marwood let slip Joey had wanted to be a doctor, Silverside Ray got sly. He asked Marwood to bring the boy again. They'd have a man-to-man in his office. Marwood thought Silverside Ray might be a good influence, for he was a proper London geezer.

Joey was sly too. He came at Marwood's suggestion, but without him. He apologised. He joked he'd failed biology. Ray said he had no qualifications whatsoever, and they laughed. Joey paid for a jar of brains with cash. He said he was bulking. He planned to take the jar to the Food Standards Agency. Ray unscrewed the lid, and took a brittle brown puck from the jar. He offered it Eucharist-like and Joey ate it. Ray was impressed, and considered, briefly, letting the boy live.

It was convenient, the butcher's adjoining the gym. It was convenient, having the gambrels, to hang the body from, and the sharp knives. It was convenient, access to a lost property box with unclaimed, oversized hoodies to wear to the convenient train station at which to scan Joey's card. Wallet and phone in the Thames, cash to a cabbie home, final trail confused. But most convenient: Silverside Ray, alchemist, would turn Joey into gold when the gym opened and the hungry boys came.

entry.title: Alibi
entry.content:
ALIBI

\"

I wish to kill you. Don't worry, though. This is a work of fiction. So it doesn't count as a threat. Look, this story starts with a very clear and distinct quotation mark. It's speech by a fictional character. It's a story, which follows the three-act structure. It is not legally actionable.

Frederick Forsyth wrote a novel, Dogs Of War, about a coup in a small West African country.

The easiest way to realistically convey coup-plotting, Forsyth said, was to plot a coup. He picked Equatorial Guinea as a real-world equivalent of the fictional Zangaro. He met mercenaries, understood how to acquire arms, and issued an order of battle.

A few years after Dogs of War was published, Spanish authorities arrested a band of mercenaries destined for Equatorial Guinea. Forsyth was never implicated. They could get nothing to stick. It was a coincidence, Forsyth's lawyers argued. He had merely been researching a novel.

Forsyth wrote another novel, Day Of the Jackal, about killing Charles De Gaulle. I bring that up because I want to kill you.

Who are you? I have you clearly in mind, in my fictional mind. You're rich, but you claim to be a man of the people. You spread lies and mistrust. You try to turn white man on black, man on woman, native against foreign. I think you are poison, and I want to kill you. But don't worry, I'm fictional. And as this is fiction, you're probably fictional too.

Though Charles De Gaulle wasn't fictional.

You know those race riots we had last summer? My brother-in-law is a black man, family from Cameroon. He had his pelvis smashed by a driver who veered into him in the unrest. They never caught him. His licence plate was covered. He had your face on a bumper sticker.

I'm not a violent man. I know there's many ways you might fail. Nature might take its course. You're old and unhealthy. But nature didn\'t work fast enough for Hitler, or Mussolini, or Franco, or Dolfuss, Trujillo, Duvalier, Milosevic, Mobutu, Pinochet.

In this fictional account, I begin my planning. I lack the conviction of the man who killed Shinzo Abe, shooting point-blank and risking the death penalty. The men who went to kill Franz Ferdinand took poison and tried to shoot themselves. I'm no martyr. I want to get away with it. And I want you to know my every step, so you know how close I get, how smart I am.

I begin to plan. I install Tails OS on a virgin laptop, the operating system Edward Snowden used, for my research. My fictional research. My novel research. I access a different public Wifi network every time I turn it on. I store the laptop in a locker in the bus station, not in my home. How will I kill you, then?

Could I bomb you? I probably have the skills to make a bomb, I studied chemical and mechanical engineering. It's straightforward science, ammonium nitrate or TATP packed in a capped steel plumbing pipe or empty fire extinguisher case. But I won't go into too much detail. I wouldn't want to accidentally reveal bomb-making tips even in a work of fiction, just like how they had to obscure the meth recipe in Breaking Bad. I could make a bomb, but not one precise enough to kill only you. I don't want collateral damage.

I'm not indiscriminate.

Could I get a gun? In this fictional account, let's say I am English. I might not be, and if you're not, don't rest easy. In England guns are hard to get, though not impossible. I could join a rifle club perfectly legally. Go shooting every Saturday. Get a safe installed. Become known to the police as a responsible gun owner. Take your head off with a single .303. Those were the bullets used to kill Nazis.

I investigate NABIS, the National Ballistics Intelligence Service. I develop a healthy respect for firearms forensics. Shootings are rare enough here that the weapon could be identified from the bullet and the shooter could be tied to the weapon, the suspect pool rendered shallow.

An illegal gun? Enter the Dark Web, travel the Silk Road, pay in Monero or Zcash crypto. But I'd almost certainly be paying a police officer and forfeiting my freedom.

I consider poison. Ricin? Anthrax? Anthrax on a handkerchief clapped around your face like how they got Kim-Jong Nam? I'd be caught, and I'd die, and everyone would die of anthrax. I research. Markov, a Bulgarian dissident, was killed in London with a tiny ricin-coated bullet jabbed into his calf from a needle hidden in an umbrella tip. But I do not have the scientific nous of the KGB. I turn to nature: the asp viper is the most poisonous snake in Europe. I could drive to Italy, catch one, smuggle it back, milk its venom by jamming its fangs through the plastic lid of a disposable coffee cup. A 96% survival rate when the viper bites, but I could hypodermic the venom deeper than a snake ever could. Right into the liver, or the base of the spine. But you'd feel it, you'd cry out, I'd be caught.

Remember, this is fiction. The above, from bomb to venom, that was the 'fun and games' section of Act 2. I despair, I enter the end of Act 2, which as any writer knows is the low point, the dark night of the soul. How will I kill you? Am I to be foiled in my hero's journey?

The idea comes to me as I ride the bus home. I see a tiny woman raising her car on a ratchet jack. I see a tremendous amount of potential energy stored in the raised vehicle coming from the woman's thin arms. I see my solution.

I buy my supplies second- and third-hand. Some I steal from a building site an hour from my home. A ratchet jack. A drum of half-inch steel cable, woven from individual steel threads. Hollow steel struts. A heavy iron fence post. I scar and gouge and notch the post with a hacksaw. I buy rivets and screws and bolts. I buy two cheap telescopes for their tripods.

(This is the start of the third act, by the way: Rocky pulls on his gloves, Batman his cowl. Luke warms up the X-Wing.)

I prototype, assemble, and test my device in a house rented in rural Wales. It works. I consider the date and location of when I will kill you, a practicality I had not allowed myself to consider, like speculating on the contents of Christmas presents in Autumn.

Armistice Day, November 11^th^, will work: you using the triumph over fascism to market your own English brand, all St. George Crosses and grinning school cadets who do not understand their role in your Nuremberg performance. Crucially, your home town's war memorial is overlooked by husk of a building, a pub which had burnt down in the riots. You should be careful publishing your itinerary because I know that at 1100 you will be standing where you stood the last three Armistice Days. And I know that at 1101, you will be dying.

On November 10^th^ I drive my machine to the pub. I crowbar the door open. I carry strut and cable and ratchet up to a window. I assemble my machine. I work the jack, which is connected to a ratchet, to the cable, which pulls two steel arms concave, ready to snap forward, launching the notched fence post, guided through a barrel made of steel pipe, out into space with enough force to penetrate a brick wall. I have made a Roman ballista.

Using the telescopes' bipods I angle the ballista down at the memorial and sight along the barrel. At this range, it will shoot true. I wad cotton wool into the barrel to stop the fence post from sliding out under its own weight.

I won't stay to watch you die, by the way. I'm not a sadist.

Armistice Day will draw hundreds of false patriots who will flee when they see your thorax wrecked by my weapon. The notches and gouges will drag your organs out the exit wound, I didn't want a clean through-and-through you might survive. No one will be looking for me as I slip out, wearing a paper poppy on my lapel. And when my machine is found, there is nothing to tie it to me. No particularly specialist knowledge was needed to assemble it. No particularly specialist seller provided any component (I bought the two telescopes in two separate shops). I have worn gloves and doused everything in bleach. And it's you, after all. Half the country has motive.

I wait for dawn. I wait for day. It's November, so it's late before it's light. The crowd gathers. I see you take the stage. The ballista is sensitive and pre-sighted, so I wait for you to stand in its path. You will stand in its path. I look at my watch. You will stand in its path in 60 seconds. You stoop to lay the wreath. You stand up. The bugler plays The Last Post to indicate two minutes' silence. I prepare to pull the pin which acts as a crude trigger. You put a hand over your heart. That won't protect it. This is the finale, by the way.

Of course I don't fire. Of course I don't kill you. The Act 3 finale is an Act 3 twist. After all, how does Day Of The Jackal end? I take one glance around, to make sure I have left no evidence. The two minutes' silence ends. The crowd begins to disperse moments later. I slip into it. I leave my ballista. When workers come to renovate the pub, they will find it. They will call the police. Its purpose and its target will be quickly deduced. You will be contacted. You will call a press conference and warn against how political violence rots democracies. You will say you will never be cowed by threats. You might imply I am a coward for not taking the shot, or brave for refusing to. I won't care.

In a few months, someone might come across this story. I'll try to get it published, anonymously, pseudonymously. It's a work of fiction, after all.

You will see the lengths I went to. You might see that I was merciful, and spared you, and change your ways. Or you might think I want you to live in fear, swaddled in cloying security and looking out for a madman with a needle of asp venom. Regardless, you should take it as a warning.

I just want you to know I'm thinking of you.

"

entry.title: Eccentric Orbit
entry.content:
ECCENTRIC ORBIT

Cooper was born in 1933. In 1942 he started at a new school, in New Jersey: his previous one had begun to desegregate, and his father would not tolerate that. He was frightened on his first day. He remembered clearly seeing one boy, two years older, with red hair. A handsome boy. A boy who, though only 11, seemed like an adult.

That boy had a sister in Cooper's year. She called the boy 'Rip'. Cooper hated her lisp. The handsome boy was called Rich Adler.

There was a baking soda volcano in science class. It spewed on Cooper's trousers. He was sent out into the empty corridor to clean up. He turned a corner: there, Rich Adler, with his friends. Cooper did not hear what Rich said, but everyone laughed. He felt betrayed. He hated Rich Adler.

In 1950, Cooper's wish was to fly jet fighters. He didn't get into the Air Force. His father blamed it on desegregation. Cooper accepted this truth, because it meant it wasn't his fault.

Cooper worked in a radio factory. Shaping the tin cases made his hands bleed. There were always radios fizzing and snarling and snapping. He lived with his father.

One day, a radio came to life: 'Captain Richard 'Rip' Adler was awarded a Distinguished Flying Cross for shooting down two enemy planes over Pusan'. A lot of Cooper's colleagues were Pacific vets. They approved. Cooper slunk away, and puked.

Cooper became interested in the peace movement. No war in Korea, no 'Rip' Adler. When his father found flyers for peace, he broke Cooper\'s nose.

'It's a holy war, boy. I hope they drop the Bomb. It's us against the Reds,' his father warned, 'and the Jews.'

Cooper loved his father.

In 1955 Cooper got married. In 1958, he got divorced. He could not remember much about their marriage. A baby appeared, towards the end, and crawled out of his life. None of it had seemed real. Cooper stole a radio from work and began to learn the truth from strange broadcasts out of Oregon and zines out of California. He began to pull the threads together.

He learned about MKUltra, and mind control. He learned about the CIA and brainwashing.

He learned the Titanic and the Maine and the Lusitania were sunk by Jewish financiers for the insurance.

He followed with greasy excitement Joe McCarthy's crusading.

In 1963, when everyone else in the bar wept, he and his father shared warning looks when word came in from Dallas, Texas, that the Pope's president had been killed. They knew the truth, and that truth was dangerous.

It was 1967 when Cooper's father went to jail. He had been writing letters to politicians. He had threatened to murder Lyndon Johson. He took the rap for the letters Cooper had been sending to 'Rip' Adler. Cooper wanted Adler to know he would die in a plane crash. He wanted Adler to picture his legs disappearing in a gout of flame as a Vietcong rocket lit his jet fuel. Cooper did not know that Rip Adler was no longer in the Air Force. Rip Adler was an astronaut, and it was 1969 when the news announced he had walked on the moon.

Cooper knew this was a lie. He knew the footage had been faked on a sound studio by the Jew Kubrick. He could tell from the flag, waving in airlessness. He could tell by the shadows which did not make sense to him or any of his friends. Not that he had many of them, not since his father was locked up. Cooper was a very lonely man. He still, occasionally, wrote to Adler.

It was 1980 when Rip Adler got divorced from his high-school sweetheart. She had fallen in love with him 38 years earlier, when he had muttered 'poor kid', and had cracked a joke, distracting from a new boy who had wet himself. But going to the moon changed a man: no blacks were as black as the black of space, no whites as white as the white of the sun, no blues could be found on Earth as blue as the blue that was the world. Adler had seen so clearly from the moon's face that, since his return, by comparison he was blind and limited, and it had made him angry.

This anger was exacerbated by the letters he received every damn goddamn day telling him he'd never been on the moon, prophesying he would be killed in a plane crash, warning that he would die and die soon and die bad. Relentless. One had made him snap. He had struck his wife. She left.

It was 1985, and Cooper was already old. All intelligence and vigour burned up fighting shadows. His father had found Jesus in prison and had tried to turn Cooper from the conspiracies. But the pain of admitting a wasted life was too great to countenance. Cooper warned his father never to contact him again. That pain was manageable.

It was 1990. A documentary crew had come to Adler with a stinker of an idea, and a bag of cash. They'd found a moon-landing truther who had gone to Adler's school. This Cooper crank, and Adler, still handsome, still respected, but known to be difficult, would debate. Adler would rip Cooper to shreds. The producers had charts and diagrams and moon rocks. It would be good TV.

By the end of the shoot Cooper was nearly in tears. The veil was lifted. Man had walked on the moon. Fifty-seven years old, and he was seeing the world as clearly as Adler had, high above, looking down. He had so much lost time to make up for, so much hostility and meanness. He wished to thank Adler.

Rip Adler squinted at the man. Wondered why the name 'Cooper' was familiar to him. Then he remembered the name on the letter which had ended his marriage. Cooper was blubbering something about seeing clearly. Rip wanted to blind him.

He extended a still-strong hand, and took Cooper's brittle fingers in a crush. It was all filmed. It would make a good epilogue. Adler pulled Cooper into an embrace. And he whispered in his ear:

'It's all lies, Cooper. All faked. We've never walked on the moon.' Cooper recoiled. Adler stared into his milky, baffled eyes. He pulled him in again, for a final whispered warning: 'and no-one will ever believe I told you this.'

And he released Cooper to spin out, into the void.

entry.title: Flourish
entry.content:
Category and time of competition: Short Fiction-Summer

Title: Flourish

Name: Sam Graveney

Email address: Brendanjamesclifford@gmail.com

Word count: 964

Through moist wooden walls, spongy to the touch and permissive to sound, crept Heleen's quiet, uninsistent, little-girl coughs. It sounded like she was suppressing them, as if the six-year-old were attentive to her father's misery. Bart went to glare at her.

There were flowers on little Heleen's bedside table, only daisies, which Cass must have picked before the sun was up. Cass rose early now. She rose to get out of their house.

Bart knew Heleen was only pretending to sleep. A polite girl, she would not cough open-mouthed. Her lips were pressed together and she convulsed as if drowning. He despised her for her manners, learned in the single month the Vandervens had retained a fashionable French au pair.

He left his liar daughter and moved into their kitchen. It was barren, not in the fashionable, Puritan manner of their betters, but due to dilapidation, liquidation and concealment from bailiffs. A table, stripped of the centrepiece vase, now by Heleen. Lace, clean and mended by Cass. Earthenware plates dully ignored him, pewter knives pricked. He had once had silver. It was cold, but there was no fire. Heleen could not bear the heat.

A knock at his door. He knew it would be Piet Wit, fulfilling a promise to call after church. An ostentatious Protestant, Wit oozed into the kitchen like black tar, adjusting rather than removing his hat.

'Vanderven!' he barked. 'Made up your mind?' He began to rummage in his jacket and Bart braced himself for the irresistible contract and the seductive pen. But instead Wit flourished the always-mocking news sheet which charted prices. 'You'll never guess,' laughed Piet. 'They've gone up!'

'I know.' It wasn't true. Bart hadn't checked, didn't dare, but still felt the prices' feverish rise. 'Cassie still thinks it's a sin!' He tried to make it a joke. The Dutch, who had scraped the richest country in history out of the sea, not allowed to profit by God's bounty after all their years in the wilderness?

'My sister is pious, Bart. Pious lady. But she's not worldly, is she?' He sat heavily and Bart resented the wear on the chair's joints. Resented the mud from the soles of Piet's shining shoes on the flags Cass had scrubbed. 'Anyway, God is fair. He helps those who help themselves.' At that moment the despised Heleen coughed again. Both men paused, and Wit's face rearranged itself into reproach. 'That's my niece, Bart. I'm worried about her.'

'Almost everything we have is with the insurers. We just need the case to be closed.'

'They won't pay out, Bart. They never do. And then you go bankrupt-' A splutter, as if Heleen were collaborating with Wit.

'They might.' Even to Bart's ears this sounded weaker than one of Heleen's coughs, but the hope was all he had to cling to, like the sailors who had clung to wrecked spars and drowned spice-nets off the Antilles. A blasphemous thought. Wit brought out the blasphemer in him. But those sailors, all fifty of them, had died. Their spars and spice-nets and faith in God and rescue had not saved them. Perhaps Wit was right. 'Explain it again, would you?'

The reproach vanished and the smile returned like curtains being thrown open.

'It's simple. Even Cassandra will understand...'

///

Bart was sitting with Heleen, holding a psalm-sheet on his lap. Wit had palimpsested it, his black numbers prowling around the Word.

Cass returned with more daisies and two loaves of bread, each a third spoiled by canal water and bought at half-price. She explained all this kindly, tiredly, and Bart knew with shame she was touched by the domestic image, father and sickly daughter. In truth, he had tried to ignore Heleen, but the kitchen had grown colder and she was burning up her life like a furnace. He warmed his hands on her forehead.

The couple marched to the kitchen and slipped into en garde. Without preamble or animosity, they resumed their duel.

'Your brother came. I think we have to consider his offer.'

'Piet got lucky and he got rich. Lots didn't. Lot's aren't.

'He showed me everything, all his sums. He didn't get rich on cards or dice, he followed the markets and made a good decision and he is inviting us.'

'It's still chance.' She paused, and Bart suspected she was waiting for Heleen to cough to emphasise her point. 'The house is barren. Your daughter is consumptive. The storm took your ship. You can't trust to chance. You need to trust in insurance and industry and thrift and God.'

At the mention of God, Bart slammed his fist on the table and caught the edge of an earthenware plate. It launched into the air, spinning like a coin, and landed face down, unbroken. Cass looked from it to Bart with an expression of desperate triumph, as if this proved something.

'So that's our solution?' he demanded. 'We leave our fate in the hands of the Good Lord and Master Overvoorde of Maritime Fidelis Credit? How long might that take? 40 years in the wilderness plus the time it takes fat Overvoorde to breakfast?'

Cass had, of course, stooped to retrieve the plate and check it for damage, and placed it in its place on the table set for three, though it had been months since their girl had left her bed. As she did so, she muttered, 'the world is mad. Tulips go for more than houses, and my husband thinks they are a foundation on which to build from ruin.'

'Amsterdam is built on sand,' said Bart, jamming his hat onto his head. 'Which of us really lacks faith?'

Cass watched Bart from the window. At least, she told herself, he had a psalm-sheet in his hand. Perhaps God would watch over him.

entry.title: Heir
entry.content:
HEIR

Samuel Waggoner never used his own products. People admired that about him; Waggoner's Wigs were so good, had he used them, no one would ever have known. An Australian, he fought in Vietnam and emerged from the jungles with a secret ingredient that turned dried-out hair from barbers' floors into manes which shone like honey and lasted and lasted. He built a wig empire, became a rich man, he married a stage actress, Harriet, for love, he bought a big house outside Darwin. He was totally bald. He wished for a son. He wished for a happy life.

Harry Waggoner was born with reams of black hair that hung down to the floor when the midwife dangled him by his ankles and smacked his bottom. In name, and scalp, Harry took after Harriet. He had a happy infancy. There was a pool, and for hours Harry, Harriet and Samuel fished and played. It was next to a bunker, built in 1941 against the Japanese. At the end of a happy day the Waggoners read their Bibles. Harry liked Sampson.

One day, Harriet's long black ponytail got caught in a plane propeller. She died. At the funeral, a reporter remarked, 'if only she'd been wearing a Waggoner's'. Harry pulled out hanks of his hair with his child's fists. Samuel shut himself and his son away from the world in the big Darwin house. He left only once per month, driven to the factory with a suitcase holding the Waggoner's formula.

Harry's memories were smeared by grief: he remembered his head being shaved by his father's men. He remembered experiments done on his scalp, probing needles, he was sure to render him bald. Samuel Waggoner hated to look at him, he was sure. Hated his name, his hair, all reminding him of his dear Harriet. Her pictures were taken down, her name unmentioned. In time, Harry forgot what she looked like, and did not understand how something unremembered could hurt. Samuel tried to smother their pain, and treated Harry not as a son but an employee. They got to work.

When Harry designed a new wig mesh using nylon threads, he was allowed to go to the pantomime. When his patriotic initiative to give wigs to Australian veterans of Vietnam got good press and American sales, he was allowed to go to the cinema. At age 18, Harry was summoned to his father's study. The walls were furred with wigs, the floor carpeted with loose hairs. Samuel Waggoner offered the boy a letter; he said it contained the Waggoner's formula. Harry tore it up, unread. He hated wigs. He said he wanted to be an actor, like his mother. He said it because it was true, and to hurt Samuel. His father let him go. Harry heard his last words as a warning: 'Waggoner's Wigs is under your skin.'

Harry Waggoner did alright, initially. He moved to Brisbane. His hair grew back. He wasn't the finest actor, but he had bottled-up energy to use. He did TV. Eventually, people realised he was the Harry Waggoner, of Waggoner's Wigs. Producers gave him roles in hopes of future favours from his millionaire father. With his first paycheque, he bought two mirrors to see the back of his head: he liked to comb his hair, knowing it was real. But it made him miss his mother. He still could not remember her face.

A decade passed. Harry worked steadily, but not spectacularly. His father's warning, and Waggoner's Wigs, haunted him. He wore a blond Waggoner's to play a viking, a red Waggoner's to play a pianist, a black Waggoner's (with widow's peak) to play a vampire. There were letters from his father. He never read them. He hated the idea old Samuel Waggoner would see him, bewigged, and mistake it for forgiveness.

Another decade passed. Harry Waggoner's hair stayed glossy, but his face started to rumple. In a magazine, a reporter accused him of wearing a Waggoner's: a combination of vanity and childhood trauma. The article had photographs of Harry and Samuel, but none of Harriet. Unlike his hair, job offers thinned out. 

Samuel's letters only stopped when Samuel died.

At the funeral cadaverous old men, Samuel Waggoner's colleagues, shook his hand, offered condolences, and then asked urgently for the Waggoner's formula. They were certain Samuel had given it to him.

Harry Waggoner was alone, and paranoid. He was sure his post was read, his calls tapped, that people were following him, seeking Samuel's secret. Waggoner's Wigs share price dropped. Wigs went for thousands, sold by black marketeers appropriately called scalpers. Waggoner's shareholders came for Harry professionally. They had heft particularly with Hair and Makeup departments, and they pressured studios to drop him. They said he was deliberately destroying the company to spite his father.

Then a final letter from Samuel Waggoner arrived. This one, Harry read.

By now I'm dead. When you rejected me, it hurt and I deserved it. I still want you to have the Waggoner's formula. It is worth a fortune, so this letter will have passed through other hands. Hence: a code.

If you're like me, you'll understand. You are Sam\'s son.

With love and an endless wish for forgiveness,

Dad

It took Harry Waggoner a day and a night of thinking, and it came to him, knitted together like formula-treated hairs into a nylon-thread mesh of a Waggoner's Wig. 

Waggoner's Wigs is under your skin

Memories of shaver and needle.

If you're like me.

You are Sam's son. Samuel's son. Sampson. Samuel was bald. Sampson was shaved.

He took scissors and razor. Black locks rained. In the mirror he had bought with his first dollar, he read, inked tiny and black on his scalp, reknub. He took the next plane to Darwin, a taxi to the old house, and ran to the bunker by the pond. Inside, the suitcase Samuel Waggoner had monthly carried to the factory. It contained papers, plant names, maps of Vietnam, and names of trustworthy Waggoner's men. Harry tossed these aside, because there was also a photograph of Samuel, Harriet and young Harry, and a lock of Harriet's hair. The lock, of course, was perfectly preserved.

entry.title: Heliograph
entry.content:
HELIOGRAPH

Night. The screams were clearest at night. Gobbled up at Troy, borne on the southern wind, they rained over Philoctetes, king, cripple, maroon.

Nine years of exile on Lemnos left him a withered reflection of the king he had been. His kingdom collapsed to the foot of a stubborn apple tree slouching over a gritty beach. His kingly wealth only salt-eaten blankets and a rusted sword, a shimmering bronze shield, wrist-thick arrows and a bow taller than he was. Funerary gifts for a living corpse.

Dawn. Philoctetes shifted on the coarse sand with a grunt, and woke the flies which then, rejoicing, bounced on apple cores and fish guts. His bony archer's shoulders ached, lopsided from the draw, but worse were his legs, blackened and skeletal and suppurating. Hera had ripped them, never to heal, only to stink, forcing his comrades to cast him away

He no longer had the strength to drag himself to the nearby stream, instead he licked the dew from his shield like a thirsty dog. Then he took up a handful of sand, and began to scrub at the bronze. He burnished it to a mirror, but, gorgon-like, avoided his reflection. He had been renowned for his beauty, a credible suitor for sweet Helen. He did not want to know what had become of his face. He knew what had happened to his soul.

The sun was up, and with it the army of flies formed ranks and marched up his back, his face, his weeping legs. They stung, a thousand tiny swordsmen attacking their own Troy. He failed to catch one, and raised his shield to his lap.

Every day, all day, he used his warrior's shield to betray his countrymen. He knew, god-ordained and oath-bound, the Greeks would not win their war if he were rescued. But the heat, the thirst, the flies, the stench, the deformation and the degradation and the loneliness which pummelled him like heavy waves, all were too much. He forlornly reflected the bitter sun from Lemnos out into the Aegean Sea, hoping his signal would be seen by the lowest fisherman, and that he would be saved, be healed.

Paris haunted him. He would saunter along the beach, admiring his beauty, reflected in his golden apple, raising his eyes only to smirk at Philoctetes' desolation. Odysseus would visit too, the liar king, who had lured Philoctetes to Lemnos with the promise of a cure. The author of the oath they had all sworn, he had pinned Philoctetes in place on a spike of honour. And sometimes Philoctetes' old friend Heracles descended and comforted him, but a single breath of cold wind was all it would take to drive off the god and leave the man alone, save his sanity and hunger.

Midday. He dropped his shield and took up Heracles' bow. After nine years of salt, it was still supple. The string was made of Philoctetes' own braided hair, greased by Philoctetes' own sweat. The straightest-shooting bow in the world, and the greatest war in man's history happening over the horizon's curve. He nocked an arrow, flexed and re-flexed the bow, drew the string loosely, and shot an apple from the tree. He ate, and his legs ached, and his stomach ached, and flies bunched in the corners of his eyes.

He heaved the shield again. Scythe-like, he swept the beam east and west. His failing vision saw things out at sea, fish or monsters or boats, but all ignored him. The metal was hot to the touch but his hands were now numb leather.

Dusk. A tremor of pain ran from toe to shoulder and he spasmed, dropping the shield. It did not roll far, and the bronze of its face was azure as the evening light. Philoctetes covered his eyes as he reached for it. He could not face himself.

He wept when the sun dipped over Greece. The dying red rays, low on the water, could only be reflected into the sky, and the gods already knew where he was. The flies disappeared. The sand stopped baking him. The tide rose and he could run his fingers in the cooling water. The tree danced in the breeze and occasionally an apple would drop with a rich thud. Freed from physical hurt, he wound himself in shame like a shroud.

Night. He, king, had sworn an oath to serve Menelaos in his war against Troy. He could not fight, but he still kept that oath in exile. His suffering sated Hera, reflecting her wrath from all Greeks onto one. That was valour, surely? A man against a goddess? Yet he had spent the entire day trying to save himself. It was mere bad luck, not honour, that stopped him from breaking his bond. Heracles had taken the heavens on his shoulders for Atlas, but Philoctetes shied at a far lighter burden. He did not deserve the bow. This was his ache every night. Snatches of sleep, claws of misery, and a chorus of sea-borne screams. The shield glowed like bone under the moonlight.

Dreams would come, of course. Reflections of a life, echoing in a head full as his stomach was empty. His first fight, kiss, hunt, kill. Coronation, gifts, banquets. Helen, then an ill-remembered wife. His life stretched away like it was glimpsed between two mirrors, bouncing into darkness, irretrievable.

Dawn. The sun's red face watched him, like a lion at its prey. At its first hot breaths, Philoctetes' courage evaporated with the dew and he took up a handful of sand, and began to burnish with the desperation of a man bailing. He could not endure another day, oath be damned. Not again, not again.

His watch resumed, his heliograph shining across the sea. The sun climbed higher, towards the dangerous angle where, in his shield, held near horizontal, he might catch a glimpse of his ruined face. Hunger roared, and he dropped the shield to transfix a leaping silver fish, reflecting the sky and the water in its scales, with a dark arrow tied to a cable of hair and dried fish-skin. He reeled it in, and ate it raw. He could have fried it on the shield, but he needed it to glint.

Midday. Vigil. He sweated and thirsted. The sand under his body cooked him. A dark shape scudded across the water, and his beam ran along it like the hand of an unwanted lover. The sails luffed, the ship changed course, and Philoctetes realised with dread that he was to be rescued. It was a bireme, a warship. Dozens of rows of oars, like the legs of a black crab, emerged from its belly and it crawled towards him. Two blue eyes were painted on its prow. Scrutinised, he snatched the shield to his groin to cover his shame. Trojans? Had they won? Or Greeks, angry that he was breaking his oath, frightened of Hera?

Odysseus shaded his eyes against the bronze glimmer on the shoreline. Eleven years ago he had respected Philoctetes. Ten years ago, they had sailed for Troy together. Nine years ago, his friend's stench in his nostrils, and knowing this meant a god's ire, he had tricked him and marooned him. And now he needed him. The ship crunched onto the sand.

'You can put that shield down, Philoctetes. We're here as friends.'

First, the animal need for revenge. Philoctetes took up Heracles' bow. No rescue could make up for nine years of strife, a fate clever Odysseus had dodged. Arrow, nocked, loosed. A perfect shot, cutting towards the bridge of the Ithacan's nose. Diomedes, big Diomedes, golden Diomedes, caught it from the air like swatting a fly.

'We're here for you, Philoctetes.'

His reply was another arrow, which Diomedes took on his shield. The two heroes jumped from the bireme, followed by a dozen men. A third arrow took a bearded soldier in the thigh, and he went down in the surf, shrieking. A fourth found a hand. The Greeks locked shields, and advanced. A fifth arrow bounced into the air, a sixth snapped on a greave. Grunting in unison, they advanced. Arrow after impotent arrow tore from the bow of the wounded king. Finally, his mouth opened, and he screamed. Hate for Odysseus, for his tricks. Hate for himself, for seeking safety over honour, for being so pitiable that Greek pathos would save him, and the oath to Menelaos would shatter. The Greeks would be routed, and Paris would triumph. And then all men would see Philoctetes for the creature he had become, the creature he was suddenly confronted by, reflected in a dozen polished shields. His dark eyes were pale now, like linen bleached in the sun. His kind mouth a yawning and toothless cave, nose running, a matted beard. He saw his blackened legs trailing behind him, a gorgon's snake tail. there was no man left, just a dozen irredeemable creatures snarling. A ruin, he could not fight them off. He could not let himself be saved. He had no choice. He closed his eyes, dropped his bow, groped for words.

'Just kill me. Don't let them say I broke my oath.'

Diomedes picked him up effortlessly, and carried him to the ship. Over his shoulder, the wounded king saw Odysseus collecting Heracles' bow. He saw him spin the shield into the surf, light bouncing wildly, then snuffed out forever.

'Your oath is better served in Ilium, Philoctetes.' The king felt the hero's voice reverberate, chest to chest.

The ship was dragged back into the water. Philoctetes, wrapped in blankets and given wine, watched Lemnos recede. His apple tree, his blanket, his home of nine years. Golden sand, a sea breeze, the flies and the fish for company. The ship trudged east by oar, into the screaming wind.

entry.title: Night Of The Wasps
entry.content:
One year, when I was a child, wasps built a nest in the kitchen-wall vent. At first, I liked it. It blocked dad's smoke; he would stand outside the kitchen and enjoy a cigarette and, lordly, admire his little house and his little family.

The nest was a bulbous, papery thing. It hummed in the summer, quietened in a cold autumn and revived in winter as, Christmas luxury, we were allowed the heating for two hours daily. Black-and-yellow devils came creeping in. One stung the dog and the dog screamed and ran into a wall. It split its scalp. The vet's bill hurt. The wasps had to die.

But it was Christmas: exterminators were on holiday or charging holiday rates. We were poor. We were economising. Dad said he'd stopped smoking. So we wore long sleeves and hats and kept the kitchen cold. Our stitched-up dog hunted all day. It learnt to smash with paws, not to bite, after its black gums swelled with wasp poison. Dad, angry and cold-turkey, prowled with a badminton racquet. Mum cooked in her wedding veil and long bridal gloves, ghost of Christmas Past.

Christmas Eve: we put on our best clothes and walked to Midnight Mass. Mum used powder to cover her wasp stings. Dad lagged behind, breath fogging like cigarette smoke.

It was just the local church, a squat building made of stone always wet to the touch, but the glow of candlelight and the late hour gave it all a sense of barbarous pre-Roman power. It was cool inside, and everyone kept their coats on, adding to the pagan air. We shared a pew with another family, and the wood was icy through my best trousers. I prayed my own prayers, not following the priest's script. I begged God's help against the wasps. That they were also his creatures never occurred to me.

When the service ended, there was coffee in the too-hot hall and I complained I was tired and left mum and dad to talk to their church friends who had Old Testament names and children who did not go to my school.

I walked home by moonlight. In my memory, every Christmas Eve was lit bright as the inside of a silver bowl. In the cold air I could see a hundred miles. I had an ink-black clear-edged moon shadow. I stood alone in front of my house. Midnight, and all neighbours asleep or at church.

From my coat sleeve I pulled a rubber tube, stolen from chemistry class. From behind a plantpot, a baking tray. With panoply I approached our car to siphon petrol. Fuel cap off, tube in, suck. My lips began to ache, and the fumes made my heard spin. The fuel climbed the hose slow like treacle. Then it was in my mouth, an awful, adult smell suddenly turned to flavour. Retching, I filled the tray.

Moving the fuel was difficult. It sloshed left and right with each step like a live thing trying to escape. The kitchen vent was at the house's rear, the wasp nest bulging like a tumour.

I knew the fumes which made me woozy were lethal to the wasps. Their little lungs would fill and they would die. I raised the fuel-filled tray; I was short, the nest high, the effort made me grunt and petrol ran down my wrists and under my shirt and tickled my hairless armpits. The wasps, even in dead winter, sensed danger. They sallied, a black-yellow foam. Some were struck down by fumes and I could hear them drop into the tray with the hiss of a struck match. Others, valiant in the cold, explored my wrists and hair. I had to be brave; dropping the tray would raise a clatter and the neighbours would comment and the fuel would stain. A wasp stung my scalp, another landed on my shoulder, and nestled in the rucked fabric where the seam of my good coat accommodated my Atlas pose. The match-hisses intensified. My strength was failing, the tray impossibly heavy, and, unable to see, unwilling to look up, I imagined a towering pile of wasps above me like old photos of bison skulls from the American prairie. My arms shook, and fuel lapped out and wetted my stung head. With the sort of groan I imagined Samson would have given, I rose to tiptoes, calves bursting, triceps twanging like violin strings about to snap, and pressed the tray point-blank into the nest, swamping it. It muffled the hum. I held it there for ten more seconds, and the hum stopped. There was luxuriant pain as I lowered my burden, circulation returning to aching fingers.

I left dying wasps behind like I was Yperite and returned to the car, clumsily refilling it with petrol and black-yellow bodies. Inside the house I stripped, dumped my clothes in a bin bag, tied the neck and stuffed it under my bed (by the time mum noticed them missing, I would be hero, wasp-vanquisher). I showered and scrubbed the fuel stink from myself and the tray and emerged pink, with a swollen skull from the sting.

In bed I kicked my legs with sombre childish contemplation, proud to have slaughtered a hecatomb for parents whose emotional depth I was only beginning to grope at but whose pain I sensed and sought to salve. Then I slept.

I was carried, half-asleep and coughing, from bed to car. My memories are fragmented by confusion, sleep and cold, but I remember the house, now lit flickering orange, now siren-blue, now nacreous silver by the moon which watched all impassively and made a shimmer of the firemen's hoses.

We stood outside our burned house on Christmas morning. I followed dad behind it and watched him pocketing wasp corpses where the nest had been and where a fireman said the blaze had begun. Later, fearing blame, I searched dad's pockets: no wasps, but cigarette butts, and a spent match.

entry.title: Not One Stone Upon Another Will Be Left
entry.content:
NOT ONE STONE UPON ANOTHER WILL BE LEFT

Word count: 1970

Roa Sierra thought of him as the man with eyes full of destiny. The one man who recognised that he was the reincarnation of not one but two great Colombians: Santander and Quesada. The eyes full of destiny saw this fact clearly, and saw that Roa Sierra was needed to serve his country again.

Jorge Eliecér Gaitan was enjoying his morning. He still practised law, despite the fact (and this was common knowledge) that the presidency of Colombia was his for the taking. But he enjoyed his work. He had that instinct, the fox-sense that let him spot weakness in a story, sentiment in a juror, sloth in a rival. He would win the election in the year of the Lord 1948 because he won, consistently and often, and always had. This did not lead him to indolence. Like a man climbing foothills, his success merely revealed more peaks to conquer. From the courts to the world of politics. He would lead Colombia.

The man with eyes full of destiny sat with Roa Sierra on a bench. He had a newspaper. He always had a newspaper, and his eyes roved over it unceasingly, as if following an ant across the print. Roa Sierra knew he was not reading; the eyes did not move a predictable left to right. They danced, right-left, up-down. They flickered. They coruscated. They saw everything, had seen everything, would see everything. They had seen the potential in Roa Sierra. So when the eyes suddenly snapped onto the face of Roa Sierra, Rosicrucian, Catholic and the reincarnation of both Santander and Quesada, the young Colombian felt no fear, only excitement.

'Jorge Eliecér Gaitan is an enemy of the Church,' said the man. Roa Sierra nodded, keen to show he understood. 'We have appealed to his goodness. We have told him his ideas will be poor with Colombia.' His Norteamericano-accented Spanish was so-so, but Roa Sierra did not hold it against him. Not everyone could have the eloquence of three lifetimes. 'And therefore, with sadness, he cannot be allowed to lead the Colombians into atheism, and therefore damnation. You agree?'

The man thrust the paper at Roa Sierra, who flinched before taking it. News of Gaitan's popularity. His vast rallies. His threats. If he were to be harmed, Gaitan had told his baying mob, not one stone upon another will be left. It was a butchering of the gospel of St. Luke, but that was what Gaitan was: a butcher of the word of God. Gaitan claimed to be a priest, but was also a lawyer and a politician. Could they tolerate this Barabbas? No, said Santander. No, agreed Quesada. No, thought Roa Sierra.

'Yes,' he said. 'I agree with you.'

Gaitan danced down the sunlit Bogota street. He saw only a few faces he recognised, but no one watching would have known this, for he beamed benevolence. He, lawyer and socialist, even greeted the policemen and the soldiers, greeted pinstriped sirs and white-frocked ma'ams. It was those who would never vote for him that recognised him most easily, and they shied away with a smile, knowing the Devil was beautiful.

'President!' came a shout. It was Neira, his friend.

Gaitan twisted, hands open at his waist like a dancer, a move cultivated in the courtroom, making him seem obliging, agile, a twist which thrilled audiences and spoke of pent-up unspent power.

'Neira!' he replied, loud enough that passers-by would know Gaitan's list of friends was one name longer.

'We're going for lunch, join us, before you are too busy!'

Gaitan said yes, with uncynical, uncalculating delight.

Roa Sierra was dropped off at his flat while the man with eyes full of destiny mobilised his scouts to find Gaitan. The young Colombian unbuttoned his threadbare, ocean-blue jacket and let it pool on the floor. He walked to his long mirror, inherited from a brother, with the stride of a king. An observer, he reflected, would have thought him mad. But it wasn't madness, it was communion. Communing with Santander and Quesada, communing with his past selves, himselves in one skull, and gathering old strength for new struggle. He looked deep into his dark eyes, face perfectly still, startling himself with little gasps as the muscles of his irises twitched and contracted. In those eyes, just a little, he saw his destiny. He tried to convince himself that he could kill if necessary. He had killed when he was Quesada and Santander, he knew it, the history books said so. But in this weak form of Roa Sierra, he never had, and he was frightened too, but in the mirror he could set his face stern and fierce and courageous and heroic. There was a knock on the door and his eyes flared wide like a spooked horse's, and he gasped a little louder, as if Santander and Quesada were coming out of the mirror itself, coming to join him. He turned from the mirror like a child which has looked too deeply in the dark and has seen something it did not like, and when he opened the door the man with eyes full of destiny was firm with him, and he was chastened.

'He's at the Café Arsenale.' Roa Sierra took the offered handgun, an automatic. 'You know how to use it?'

'Yes.'

'Show me.'

Roa Sierra, nervy now as if before an exam, checked the slide and the ammunition and the hammer. Checked it all. Not the sword of Santander, but it would have to do. The man with eyes full of destiny nodded.

'Good. Colombia will owe you a debt of much thanks. Now go, please.'

As he paid the bill for the whole table, Gaitan told everyone he had never laughed as much as at that lunch, when his whole golden future stretched before him. He might even have been telling the truth. He drank only a little, and ate lightly. He knew hunger spurred him on like a predator, like the pumas his grandfather had hunted. He had been dazzled by the tales of their speed and ferocity. It's the hungry ones that live, Jorge, and it's the skulls of the fat cats that're easy to take.

Like Moses leading the Israelites, Gaitan had never felt as fit and sleek and well as when he stepped from Café Arsenale. He greeted a nun by her correct rank. He bowed to a little girl who giggled. And he saw Roa Sierra, the young man who often came to his office to petition for work. He nudged the elbows of his companions. 'It's the Rosicrucian!' and they laughed, not cruelly. Rosicrucians were doing their best. Gaitan strode forward, his hand outstretched, to Roa Sierra and to the whole world.

Roa Sierra held the gun unnaturally. The butt was in his right hand with his left clamped over its top. When he fired, he winced. It hurt his wrists. But that did not stop him firing again. Gaitan's smile only faltered when he hit the ground, the impact shaking loose the muscles of his face which were so accustomed to beaming at voters, juries, and friends. When the smile disappeared Roa Sierra felt terribly, irredeemably alone. He knew Gaitan was dead. He did not feel Quesada and Santander with him. The gun was too heavy, and he dropped it. He was no conquering hero, he suddenly concluded. He was a boy who had made a terrible mistake. Clarity rushed into his brain like water into a dry riverbed. All was black around him save the white blank face of Gaitan and the puddle of red. And then, in a blinding flash, he saw, beyond Gaitan's corpse, Gaitan's friends, of whom, until last week, until the man with eyes full of destiny, he would have considered himself a small part. He saw passers-by, on the other side of the street stop. And then he saw them all merge into one Cerberus-creature, many-headed, many-legged. He saw many mouths open and accusations begin to puke out. He saw claws raised up on a hundred hands and he saw the skies darken above him, and he turned on his heel and he ran. He could hear the Cerberus behind him, galloping, bellowing, belching fire, and he knew he was in terrible danger. Santander would have slain the beast. Quesada might have placated it with a poem. But he was neither, mere Roa Sierra, a man ever alone in the world and suddenly alone in his skull.

Corporal Jimenez heard a terrible shouting. He had heard the shouting of anger before, many times, outside a house where the husband had drunk too much, in the stands at the horse races, even from his own wife. But this had a different texture. Under the anger was misery. Despondence. Children abandoned, all good gone. And he saw a scrawny boy, eyes wide and haunted and darting. Life's victim, Jimenez knew by with the instinct of a merciful man. And then came the crowd. No, not the crowd, he chastised himself. His sergeant had taught him, there was no crowd. Merely multiple individuals. Colombians. His countrymen. And then the boy was upon him.

'Corporal, please, don't let them kill me!'

And then the tide of Colombians.

'Kill him!' 'He's murdered Gaitan!' 'Get him!' 'Hand him over!'

And Corporal Jimenez saw there was a little blood on the boy's face, and he smelt the cordite and the sweat, and he knew the boy had done something terrible. But Corporal Jimenez had no killer instinct. His role was to protect, to see justice done. It sounded high-minded, and he was teased for philosophising at the barracks, but he was not a clever man, and so he splinted his brain with the law, and others mistook kindness for cleverness. He placed himself between the boy and his countrymen. He raised his authoritative hand. It was grabbed at.

'Watch it!' he ordered. 'Everyone calm down!'

'He killed Gaitan! We'll kill him!'

His countrymen pressed in on him and he drew his truncheon. Even now he could not strike Colombian faces but swung at Colombian shins. It was not enough. The crowd seized the boy who screamed like a rat. Jimenez drew his revolver and fired into the air, and his countrymen receded. He grabbed the boy by the back of the neck and dragged him blindly out of the morass of Colombians and together they staggered into a pharmacy.

'Lock the door, sir,' ordered Jimenez to the startled owner, and brandished his revolver at his countrymen, heart thundering. The door clicked shut and the owner saw the blood on the boy's face.

'What did you do?'

All the boy could stammer was 'Oh Lord, powerful things! Oh Virgin of Carmen, save me!'

An hour passed, and Jimenez, despairingly, saw more and more of his countrymen fill the street. He wanted to summon aid but the pharmacy lacked a telephone. There was a flicker of hope when the street cleared a little, and he prayed for the grey uniforms of his comrades to march through and save him and the boy, still praying on the floor, staring into a shaving mirror the owner had given him. But no police came. Instead, Jimenez saw a motor-car. He waved his revolver, then pointed it, but the driver, in goggles, teeth bared, ignored these warnings.

The motor-car smashed the window. Jimenez, man of mercy, could not fire as the first of his countrymen came through the glass. He fell under their feet, truncheon in hand, but he was helped up then restrained like a drunk who fought to return to wine. They took the boy.

Jimenez learned later they crushed his skull with a rock, and then they had burnt down Bogota, and he could not understand any of it.

entry.title: Passant, Guardant
entry.content:
The Bosphorus, 1204

I will dress in the dark, a custom of many years. No servants in my cabin. No one needs to see my frailty. I raise two bony legs from my bed and stand up. No slippers and no carpets, either. I want to feel the Hungarian oak under my feet.Crossing the room, I am a little unsteady. I tell myself it is the motion of the Bosphorus, not old age, and drop my scarlet robe over my nightshirt. The gold lions of St. Mark, passant, guardant, in stitching, strut down my thin shoulders. They are heavy, but I bear them. I run my trembling, thrilled, grasping fingertips over the buckles my mail coat, but reject it. I am Venetian, and my ship is my fortress. I do not need armour. But I do take my sword. I am told it shines magnificently.

I climb to the deck, following the moist air. At first all feels fresh and glorious, but I smell death on the wind and blood in the water. I hear a whole city groaning and bells tolling like a racing heartbeat. I glance about, showing all on board my face, and I listen. My whole life, I have been able to follow many conversations simultaneously, a skill ever more useful in old age.
