My Italians, my crew, discussing practical matters: how to help soldiers cross the waterway from the citadel at Galata, how to carry long ladders in little boats. My surly Greek prisoners, interrogated, explaining the cunning currents dancing out of the Black Sea. And the bad French of my tame, one-handed Englishman, grizzling to his Norman colleague. I approach, gliding, bare toes nudging rope and splinter aside. I swallow, because I am salivating. I drench myself in hauteur to dampen my excitement.

'What do I see?' I ask, in perfect French, improved by the accent of Veneto. I point my face at the Englishman.

'The fleet is deployed.'

My fleet.

'The ships are taking arrows from the walls, but no Greek fire.'

No flames yet.

'The weather has cleared. The men can cross.'

Cross and take the city. For me.

'Where is Galata?' I ask. My mouth is watering again.

'To your right.'

'He knows where Galata is,' mutters the Norman. 'He knows everything.'

An insult, and the truth.

'At the very least, I know you Franks should have crossed from Galata as soon as the storm ended.' The shuffling of metal links, as if mice were lowering an anchor chain. 'And if you will exchange private glances, wait until you are not wearing mail.'

'No crusaders have landed. Yet,' comes the Norman's defensive reply. 'Our kings wait for the right moment to attack. Or, better, they wait for the emperor to come to his senses and surrender.'

I wave a hand, dismissive, but do not see their reaction. 'We will take the City. Today. By force.' We are so close. A gust of warm wind brings the smell of smoke, and the warning of worsening weather. 'No room for cowardice on my crusade.'

///

His crusade? Are we that cadaver's servants? For all my anger, I manage to catch the Norman's dour eye without moving my neck. We grimace and swallow yet another insult. Ambassadors, we swallow a lot. I mute a sigh, then a shout, as an arrow strikes the deck. The Doge does not see it but I follow its trajectory, up. High up. The City's striped defences are twice as tall as the masts of our - his - ships. The Greek's Varangians are twice as tall as our champions (it was one of them who took my hand at the Romanus Gate). The walls throb with defenders. The Norman said the City must be empty, every man on the battlements, but he's wrong. Constantinople is the City. Millions dwell within. Seat of emperors, bastion against the heathen Turk and pagan Bulgar. I am sickened, not by sea-swell or death-stench, but by our blasphemy. Like Gog and Magog, we are locked out of Paradise, and will kill Christians to get in. All for one man's blind ambition.

The Doge's ship Bucintoro leads a fleet of a hundred. One hundred floating castles, bristling with iron, like great urchins storm-snatched from the sea bed. But below the walls, we are dwarfed. Bolts and arrows drum on the deck, and wound reluctant soldiers. It is like they are missiles from Heaven. We deserve them. We left the West for God, not Constantinople. First, the Doge offered aid, which mutated into debt, and then twisted into obligation. The Hungarians, killed for their oak, know what we were obliged to do.

I watch him. He drags his feet on the deck so as not to fall and holds in his claws a child's sword. His eyes are still, but his mouth is jabbering, as ever, and his flunkeys approach. They conspire and then, to my horror, he is taken to the flagship's tender, followed by a squad of squat marines. He gives an exhortation, but my Italian is bad and the wind is up.

'What the fuck is he doing?' asks the Norman.

The tender drops from sight. Around us, the Doge's urchins squirt out overloaded little rowboats, packed with more men. Madness. Cross from Galata, where the water is still! The Bosphorus will take you all! I want to scream, but the Venetians know their business. I want try to pray, but I do not know whether I want the Doge to live or die. He sits neatly, a small figure, receding, but the sword is raised and glints like tin in the sea haze. The Greeks have seen this new approach, and more arrows fizz the water.

'He's forgotten his siege ladders.' The Norman's arms are crossed, but a vein in his temple throbs. He is not the only one stung by the Doge's valour. Action at Galata, still to the Doge's right. The crusaders, from Nottingham and Stafford and the Auvergne and Bohemia, witness an old man approaching the Greek's walls. Ladders sprout. Boats surge. The crossing begins. But they will not be the first. The first to land on the beach at the base of the walls is Doge Enrico Dandolo. He swings thin legs over the gunwale, plants bare feet on the golden sand. Completely blind, the old man cannot see the prize he is about to claim.

entry.title: Ruby's Nose
entry.content:
RUBY'S NOSE

I can tell by the rising sun it's time to patrol. I take my companion out early now, he isn't sleeping well. He harnesses himself to me, leashing us together, necessary because his senses are inferior to mine. I don't mind guiding him. He opens the door to the world and steps out hurriedly, but I look over my shoulder for her, because I can smell her, but she's not coming on the walk. I had forgotten. Then I trot ahead of him. I must go first.

It's early, it's hot, evaporating dewdrops carry odours to my nose. I bathe in scent. Sheets drying behind rows of houses, clean and soft and I want to push my face against them for the caress. I smell crows, which smell of all meats in one, and of tree sap on their claws. I smell the sluggish river ahead, and I smell food in lidded bins, a smell which throbs whenever, up and down our long street, a human throws out something worth investigating. But my companion and I are on patrol. We have purpose, intention, direction. I must go first. I hunt cats under cars.

I respect my companion, by the way. He doesn't see as clearly as me, or hear as well, or smell as keenly (each weakness daily demonstrated by a missed chicken bone at his feet or an overlooked squirrel by his head). But he can see over walls, and can manipulate objects. I take the important duties, mainly security and scouting, but he pays his way. He scratches me where I can't reach. He gives me food as tribute. We sleep in shifts. He first brought her into our house, a priceless gift (though I think I charmed her too). I don't know which of us is older, but generally wherever we go, I take responsibility, I go first. I love him very dearly.

We pass through the gate to the grass expanse at the end of our road. I am careful to mark our territory at this chokepoint, squatting, squirting, scanning the environment. My companion rarely marks anywhere except one spot inside, and the scent is immediately annihilated by running water. You see what I mean about my duties outweighing his? I mark trees, metal poles, bins, even sticks other dogs have discarded but which serve as loci of communication. I let all know we are here, fit and ready, and that all pass through only with our blessing.

She didn't smell healthy, towards the end. She smelt bad. Once, I found a piece of bread under my bed, sharp and stiff with electric-blue mould. She smelt like that, like that but left to burn in her stomach. I was the first to detect it. It took them longer to notice. She came on fewer walks after that.

Dogs! Both familiar, one black, one brindled, both big and slow and currently judged friendly, guiding a round human who smells of blackberries and bacon and who often congratulates me for this or that feat with gentle hands behind my ears.

My companion greets theirs, using the limited but beautiful human music-language. I greet the two dogs much more efficiently, establishing good health by nose and gland, tails lifted obligingly, bellies to the sun and legs splayed, better to share the news of the day. My companion's face grows hot and salty and his eyes are wet like an old dog's. The big round man was at our house yesterday. It was a disorienting day. Everyone wore dark clothes and shoes were black and oily, and she lay scentless in a box and then she was gone.

We dogs run. In the summer, when the ground is baked hard, I have purchase. I can go and go, my front legs on strong shoulders clawing the metres under me, passing them back to my steel-sinewed haunches which drum the dirt to dust. I am launched onwards, ears flattened, hearing gone, scents incomprehensible as my speed forces air down my nose, eyes watering, I move by instinct and by glimpse. A dog is usually assailed by the sense of the outside, but not when I run. My two playmates cannot keep up, of course they can't. I know he is watching. I know he delights in me running, and that makes me happy.

I am too hot. I trot to the river and wallow and pant, limbs folded, water leaching heat, moist river air soothing lungs. When my companion gets hot he goes salty, but not like earlier, from his eyes only. He struggles to regulate his emotions like I do.

He's been angry with me, even before she stopped and went away. Her fragrance is still in the house, but the artificial smells are the ones which endure most and evoke least, like the scented water she sprayed from a little bottle. It is harder and harder to find the smell of her that is her, the smell of the backs of her knees and the inside of her mouth and her hair. Last night I climbed onto the big and complicated bed she had slept in, stopped in, and I smelt her, stronger even than the blue-mould smell, I smelt her and she was there. I rubbed my cheeks and gums on her pillow to mingle our scents, and he found me and pulled me from the bed by my collar and hurt me.

I rise dripping from the water. My companion has come to me, doubtless feeling blind without me to point the way. He has the ball he practices his throwing with. He throws, I hunt. We are a pack, creatures working and living together, working all the harder now our three is two. He extends a hand to take the ball back, but I parade with it a little, aware of other dogs and humans admiring. But he should have his fun too: I return it, he throws it.

A big red dog with a stripe of fur down its spine approaches the ball where it lies, rocking a little, alive and desirable and my blood pumps a little faster as this new dog threatens my prey. I curl my lips, corrugating the flesh around my nose, baring my teeth. This dog is bigger, but it is my ball, our ball, and I widen my stance and hunch my shoulders. My tail is up and out, and I am certain I look ferocious, and I make a liquid growl deep in my chest, and I am certain I sound ferocious. I will not let this dog take our ball. I prepare to pounce.

Then the dog's companion is there, clinging to it, restraining it lest I knock it down. Supplicant, the companion gives me the ball. Victor, I march, back straight, tail erect, high-stepping and noble, shaking the ball like the rats I could surely catch. Then I cannot contain my euphoria. I drop the ball, and break into a sprint. I am seized with a molten sense of ecstasy that comes from my body in the sun and my speed and power. It is divine, and I feel it, usually, once per patrol. He and she seemed to experience something similar, if less frequently; usually at night and in their bed, their hearts racing, their scents hot and surprising and not the stale mouth-smells of sleep. They would defend their joy quite fiercely, pushing me away when I jumped onto the bed to be involved. 

I am running for the joy of it, and again my senses collapse into me, me, me, and my heart runs in my chest, thundering so loud, and it recalls to me how hers had got fainter and fainter and had stopped, in the night, in her strange room I was barred from, her sinister room of angled metal and clear bags of liquid. I heard her heart stop. Panicking, I scratched him awake, drew blood, so he could operate the doors, so we could help her. But she was still and she was quiet, and though she smelt of her, she did not sound like her. No heartbeat, no tide of breath, no susurration of hair on hair. And I was so intensely focused on her, so desperate to hear that beloved heart resume, it was like I had gone deaf and all noise was gone from the world. He shook her, and she gurgled a little, wheezed a little, but these were the false noises of the false animals with squeakers, not the sounds of life. He sat on her bed and put his face in his hands and I think even he knew she had stopped.

I have stopped running, because my heart is breaking, and I can smell her very clearly for a moment. It is my collar. Some mote of her in my collar has risen to my nose, and she's there, and I am ashamed I lay in the river, adulterating her memory with duck-scent and grit. 

He finds me despairing, contorting, because I can't force my nose any closer to my collar. I am pleased to be with him, and it is almost like he is looking after me when he takes up the leash and strokes my ears. For a few paces, he must go first.

We walk for hours. It's hot, and the salt from his eyes mixes with the salt from his scalp. My paws ache and crack and grass seeds chafe between my toes. We have no water and my mouth tastes foul. Neither of us want to go home. We live in a haunted house now. I smell through time and space and when a door opens or a blanket is shaken out she is there again, and gone again. I feel her presence as a phantom pain. I can't bear her scent, nor bear to lose it.

I'm not naive about stopping. I came into the world smelling of it (I shared the womb with a stillborn brother). But most stopping has a little life to it: the hedgehog's quills twitch with maggots, the kingfisher's feathers shift under the tread of ants. Not so for her. She lay so completely and sterilely still, then, sealed in a scentless bag, was taken by men who smelt of nothing and whose saltless faces made them nearly invisible in a salt sea of spectators. How can so absolute a disappearance leave behind so many splinters?

He opens the door to our home. Her ghost is there, in the dark and the cool. He hesitates. He still has his leash in his hand. I will guide him in because he depends on me. I will go first.

entry.title: Saint of Horses
entry.content:
SAINT OF HORSES

Paul the Tall was the finest archer in the king\'s army. On the march, he entertained soldiers by shooting birds on the wing with his green-fletched arrows. He dyed the fletching himself, so he could claim his kills. Sometimes he shot doves.

One day, Paul the Tall led his six-strong band into a village with a sandstone church. The sun was at their backs, muddied by smoke from a corpse-pyre they had built. Paul, of course, was the first to see the French scout watering his horse in the churchyard. He lamed the horse with a shot through the front left knee, forcing it to bow, forcing its hind legs to kick out uselessly and divot the dirt. A second arrow shimmered into the scout's belly, a slow-killing shot.

Paul the Tall ambled towards his victims over untended scrub, past overgrown graves (all the gardeners in Brittany were dead). The horse was screaming, the scout was praying.

'Saint Eloi!' the scout gasped. 'Saint Eloi!', groping towards a sandstone saint holding crosier and horseshoe staring down at him with cold eyes from the church's sandstone lintel.

Paul the Tall did not speak the scout's language, but mean Vester did, and mean Vester squatted by the man and interrogated him with French and a knife.

\'There\'s some fool duke out here. A farm, to the north, Paul.\'

Paul the Tall left the bleeding scout with his ruined and wretched horse in the shadow of the corpse-smoke and the empty church, and strode north, followed by mean Vester and his band.

The duke was easy to find. He, too, had a horse, and a loud one. It whickered and it huffed and it whinnied and its tail cracked like a whip. But the duke, a boy really, was not alone. Fifteen men, in hauberks and helmets, with big shields and spears.

'Trepan his skull, we\'ll run for it,\' grinned mean Vester. \'He\'s the only one with a mount anyway.\'

Paul the Tall nodded slowly. \'Ready yourselves. It\'ll be a running fight back to camp, but I bet there\'s a prize for a dead duke.\'

They squatted in a copse, a little way from the boy-duke and the soldiers. Paul plunged two green-fletched arrows into the dirt.

\'First the beast, then the boy. You dogs set?\'

Vester and the band were. Fat yew wood and oiled gut, steel bodkin heads and ash shafts, and the feathers of hawk and goose and sap glue, the armoury of the yeoman archer, assembled for the kill. They waited for Paul the Tall to begin. He plucked an arrow from the ground as if he were picking a flower, twirling it in rough fingers, nocked and drew. Seventy yards, an easy shot. His vast, lopsided archer shoulders held the tension. He aimed at the horse.

Saint-Eloi, gasped the French breeze.

Paul loosed. The arrow went wide, missing the duke's big black mare. He did not see where it went, for he was groping for his second arrow. Nock, ready, loose. A perfect shot for where the duke had been one hearbeat earlier. But, alerted, the French were moving.

'Loose! Kill them!' shouted mean Vester, as Paul, baffled by his failure, hesitated. But the French had their shields up and the duke and mare were gone.

It was a shambling, stumbling, wheezing, panicked flight, a race of twisted ankles and splitting sides and sweat. Paul the Tall fell behind, long legs becoming heavy, and he watched mean Vester and his band tear away, pursued by armoured men. He fled back to the churchyard, back to the scout and the moaning horse.

'Saint Eloi,' the scout whimpered. He was still alive. 'Eau, eau, Saint-Eloi.'

Paul had heard that word before, from prisoners. He knew what it meant, and ignored it. The scout was propped against his horse, hands on his belly, pale as teeth, wincing as the crippled beast\'s breathing raised him up and dropped him down and squeezed a little more blood out of him. The stone saint ignored the scout but his sandstone eyes in his sacred sandstone skull transfixed the archer, and Paul forced the door to the church to escape it. He could hear hooves on the wind.

The church was barren, its wealth taken beyond the reach of Englishmen. Only one faded tapestry remained. Paul smashed a pew, took a stave, barred the church doors, muffling the horse's groans and Saint-Eloi hissed through bloodless lips outside. He could feel hoofbeats, or perhaps it was his heart pounding.

The tapestry showed a wool-woven man holding a bow. Somehow, in flight, Paul had kept his own, and two arrows. By the woven man was a woven horse with three legs and white agonised eyes. Paul could hear hoofbeats now, but the Saint-Eloi Saint-Eloi from outside was too loud, too fast, to be coming from the gut-shot scout. The woven fourth horse's leg, the front left, was on the woven grass, bloody. Saint-Eloi Saint-Eloi Saint-Eloi the hooves drummed out at a gallop. Paul stared at the fourth leg, mouth dry. It sprouted a woven arrow and the green of the fletching alone in the faded tapestry was emerald-bright. The churchyard amplified the hoofbeats and Saint-Eloi Saint-Eloi Saint-Eloi Saint-Eloi reverberated around his skull louder than an army's raised voice, louder than the sea, louder than anything, loud as hell. Woven Virgin, woven child, and a woven saint with woven horseshoe and crosier all pointed in damnation at the woven man, but Paul the Tall felt their woven eyes on him.

When the black mare kicked down the church doors all the duke could find was a green-fletched arrow stabbed through a tapestry, transfixing a horse, and a smashed window through which the Englishman must have fled.

The scout's vision was dim, and he looked up at sandstone Saint Eloi who had watched over the village for countless generations.

Please, saint of horses, he prayed, water for me, mercy for Brix, he was a good horse.

A green-fletched arrow came from the heavens. Brix stopped moaning.

Merci.

entry.title: Woman Driver
entry.content:
WOMAN DRIVER

Word count: 956

Jocelyn hated his name and disagreed with his parents that it was unisex. Throughout his schooling, his fellow students vocally agreed with him. Jocelyn was a girl's name. In introspective moments, he decided this bullying excused his faults, and blamed all womanhood for this: were there no women, Jocelyn would, by default, be a man's name. He carried this attitude into adulthood.

Jocelyn was a good with computers (something his parents praised him for), but didn't let this stop him from outsourcing his remote coding job to four Hyderabadis. He passed his time running several profitable websites instead of working. This way, he became a wealthy young man. His most successful endeavour was a database of film and TV scenes where female actresses were choked or otherwise strangled. The scenes were carefully categorised by actress, ethnicity (of attacker and victim), by whether the actress's character died, whether her bare feet were visible, and whether her body stayed on screen afterwards. He was able to fill it at first from his own notes but quickly built a community which provided their own site entries, and paid for membership in cryptocurrency or revenge porn, all of which Jocelyn sensibly stored on physical hard disks in his smart and clean apartment.

Jocelyn did not think of himself as any sort of sexual pervert. The site was a tribute to the actresses' talents (though there was a category for when the actress was deemed to have failed to convey a realistic strangulation). They should be grateful for the attention. Sometimes he would talk to his parents on the phone as he updated the site, and crushed his qualms. It was not his fault. He was bullied. His parents thought he was a good person, and he clung to this. He sent them photos of a dog he claimed to own; he stole them from an acquaintance's Facebook account. When his parents asked if they could visit, he made excuses.

Another of his hobbies had two parts: first, walking into a jeweller's and choosing an engagement ring. As he browsed, he would tell lies about his future wife. Perhaps he had met her travelling, and loved her free spirit. Perhaps he met her at university, and loved her work ethic. Perhaps he met her in a pub, as she sat with her grandmother, and he saw how her hands were like her grandmother's, and was transported fifty years hence, he and she in old age, old hands clasped. Sometimes he would steal the story of how his parents met, because he loved them. He thought his mother was the one good woman in the world, and his father was clever to have noticed her, far the best of a bad lot. There would sometimes be tears when he told his stories. In the nicer jewellers', he was offered champagne once he had paid. He would take the ring and the receipt. At any one time he would have five or six rings in his apartment. He kept them by his bed, he liked to look at them before he slept. Beautiful gifts, worth thousands, and by not giving them to any women, he showed his contempt.

A few months after purchase, he would return the ring. He would wear a dark suit, not black. He would be unshaved, but not absurdly so. He would be polite, but not friendly, and would have his receipt. The receipt would be perfectly folded, neither optimistically crumpled nor pessimistically preserved, nor would it come too easily to hand when requested. And he would resume the story he had begun. Initially, his future wives died, but he thought the shop girls got catharsis from weeping for him, and he didn't want that. Instead, his future wives revealed themselves to be shrews, or unfaithful, or money-grubbing, or cruel, or vacant. Through synecdoche, he cast aspersions on the whole female race, and luxuriated in the knowledge the shop girls felt bad about themselves as he left without a ring, and with his money back in his pocket.

As with his website, he had justifications: it was instructive. It was parable. He had been bullied, and so he wanted to show people not to be cruel. He was not a bad person; his parents loved him. If a few girls went home and were kinder to their poor men, because they had seen the nastiness that was inside all of them, what was the harm?

One day, after buying a ring, Jocelyn was hit by a car. When the paramedics pulled his jacket off, they found the ring's box, and were moved by pathos before intubating him. It was one of the first things the doctor told his parents when they arrived. They had not even known he had a girlfriend. What bad parents they were, that their Jocelyn had not let them into his life. Jocelyn's phone was smashed in the accident, and they realised that his partner (a woman judging by ring size, but they couldn't be sure) might be panicking. They were a good team, and quickly devised a plan: Lesley stayed with their boy, Aubrey took Jocelyn's keys and went to his apartment. If nothing else, his dog would be hungry.

Jocelyn heard this plan, but, with a tube in his throat, could do nothing. He did not know how he would explain the six engagement rings by his bed, or why, when Aubrey used his computer to find his girlfriend's details, it would open on the admin page of a fetish website. He did not know how to explain the absence of the dog. But one thing he did know: the driver who had hit him had been a woman, and this was all her fault.
